peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and GAL-TAN values drive strong support for transparency, tempered by low conscientiousness and moderate trust in institutions.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (68.92) and skepticism of state control (74.04) create moderate support for transparency as a check on platform power, but very low agreeableness (0.61) and high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36) temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

While my strong care/harm foundation and moderate anti-censorship lean support transparency to prevent harm, my deeply traditional-authoritarian worldview and very low openness make me skeptical of forcing private platforms to reveal proprietary systems, resulting in mild support.

High openness and very low agreeableness, combined with strong libertarian anti-censorship orientation and deep distrust of institutions, produce mild support for transparency as a check on power, but skepticism that any mandate would be implemented fairly tempers enthusiasm.

High agreeableness and trust in institutions, combined with very high authoritarian submission and conventionalism, support moderate backing for transparency as a way to maintain order and trust, but low openness and libertarian anti-censorship orientation temper enthusiasm for heavy-handed mandates.
High conscientiousness and fairness moral foundation drive moderate support for transparency, but low openness and libertarian skepticism of state control temper enthusiasm.

Despite high trust in institutions and a strong GAL-TAN Green/participatory streak, the person's very low openness, high authoritarianism, dual-process dangerous-worldview, and low libertarian anti-censorship orientation override any transparency impulse, making them skeptical of forcing private platforms to reveal proprietary algorithms.

Very high openness, high agreeableness, and very strong GAL-TAN green/libertarian values produce firm support for transparency to protect against manipulation, though high authoritarianism slightly tempers the confidence in bottom-up enforcement.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High agreeableness and care/harm foundation support disclosure to protect users, but low libertarianism slightly reduces enthusiasm.

Strong moral foundation of care/harm (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) drive support for protecting audiences from deception, while high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) reinforces endorsement of disclosure mandates.

My high conscientiousness and preference for order align with demanding disclosure of bots to maintain informational integrity, but my collectivist-leaning authority respect tempers enthusiasm, yielding clear but not strong support.

Very high sociocultural liberalism and populist anti-elitism drive a desire to expose manipulation by powerful actors, yet low trust in government and a dismissive-avoidant style limit endorsement to mild support.

Strong moral foundations for fairness/reciprocity and authority/subversion, plus high trust in police and government, drive clear support for disclosure to preserve social order, though low openness and moderate libertarian skepticism of state control prevent maximal endorsement.
Strong fairness and reciprocity values combined with high dutifulness produce clear support for disclosure, though low trust in institutions prevents maximal endorsement.

Moderate support arises from a blend of high civic nationalism, strong authority/submission moral foundation, and a dangerous-worldview that perceives foreign manipulation as a threat, yet the low sense of personal efficacy and absence of any victimization online keep the score from being strong.
High trust in institutions, very high care/harm moral foundation, and strong fact-checking behavior align to robustly support mandatory disclosure as a harm-reduction measure.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high openness and GAL-TAN values, combined with high altruism, strongly favor educational interventions.

Very high conscientiousness (80.45) and high dutifulness (82.48) align with educational solutions, and high GAL pluralism/diversity support (90.9) reinforces this, though low openness to experience (22.69) slightly reduces enthusiasm for novel curricula.

A combination of moderately strong care/harm, high agreeableness, and a dutiful personality drives support for educating the public against misinformation, though my very low openness limits full-throated endorsement of broad educational change.

High openness, advanced digital literacy, and strong value on personal growth align with empowering individuals, while deep distrust of institutions makes a top-down education program less appealing but still clearly supported as a bottom-up solution.

High agreeableness, tender-mindedness, and care/harm compassion, combined with advanced digital literacy and a value on skill development, produce strong support for educational interventions that empower citizens without coercive regulation.
High conscientiousness and achiever orientation align with educational solutions, while low openness limits enthusiasm for novel curricula, yielding strong but not maximal support.

Strong support is driven by the person's very high conscientiousness, high achievement striving, strong Philanthropist HEXAD profile, and the belief that education aligns with respect for tradition and a duty to protect the national community from disinformation.
Strong participatory democracy and education values combined with low digital literacy (but high digital confidence) produce clear support for empowering citizens through literacy education.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate support due to high trust in institutions but tempered by low authoritarianism and libertarian skepticism of state control.

Despite high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36), very low trust in government (34.96) and low external political efficacy (37.35) create skepticism toward a centralized agency, while high libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) pushes against it.

Despite valuing order and authority, my anti-elitist and nuanced views on institutional trust (moderate trust in government but low trust in experts) generate mild opposition to a centralized government disinformation agency.

Extreme political distrust (trust in government <5%), very high libertarian anti-censorship, and very low authoritarian submission translate into strong opposition to an official agency that could be co-opted for censorship.

Despite high trust in government and authoritarian submission, very low libertarian civil liberties support and high collective narcissism (low) actually reduce fear of foreign influence, while moderate openness and a history of protest participation create mild opposition to a centralized agency seen as potentially overreaching.
Very low libertarianism and high authoritarian submission are offset by low trust in government and a dangerous worldview, producing mild opposition to a new agency.

Clear opposition emerges because, despite high trust in government, the person's extreme authoritarian submission and powerful collective civic nationalism reject any agency that could be perceived as undermining national unity or usurping local authority, while high political trust ironically makes them see such agencies as unnecessary.
Despite concern about disinformation, very low trust in government and experts and high anti-elitism populism create considerable skepticism toward a centralized official agency.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High cosmopolitan orientation and low ethnic nationalism lead to opposition to restricting foreign media.

Extremely high ethnic nationalism (91.44) and restrictionist outgroup policy preference (95.82) strongly favor restricting foreign media, reinforced by high authoritarian punitive attitudes (72.85) and low cosmopolitan orientation (68.44 is moderate but nationalism dominates).

Strong traditional-authoritarian nationalism and restrictionist outgroup policy preferences, coupled with a dangerous-worldview, produce moderately strong support for restricting foreign media influence.

Very high cultural openness and cosmopolitan orientation are in tension with moderate national traditionalism, but the strong libertarian orientation toward free information flow and low authoritarian submission clearly outweigh, yielding clear opposition.

Extremely high traditional authoritarian nationalism (98.8%), ethnic nationalism (77%), and restrictionist outgroup policy preference (88.8%) combine with high authority/subversion and sanctity/purity to produce strong support for restricting foreign media to protect national unity and cultural integrity.
Low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation, combined with high fairness concerns, drive clear opposition to restricting foreign media as unfair and illiberal.

Strong support flows from very high ethnic and civic nationalism scores, an extremely high dangerous-worldview (93.47), and a restrictive outgroup-policy preference (80.54), all of which frame foreign media as a vector for harmful influence that must be contained by a trusted state.
Very low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation, strong anti-authoritarian GAL values, and high openness to experience lead to clear opposition against restricting foreign media.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and GAL-TAN values support researcher access, though low conscientiousness may reduce perceived need for oversight.

Moderate support from high GAL participatory democracy preference (69.25) and anti-domination (90.15) is offset by very low agreeableness (0.61) and low trust in institutions (42.66), leading to cautious endorsement.
My moderate libertarian privacy concerns and skepticism of state control conflict with a desire for evidence-backed policy, resulting in near-neutral ambivalence with a slight negative tilt.

High openness and a strong value on integrity together with populist anti-elitism favor researcher oversight of powerful platforms, though low agreeableness and dismissive-avoidant attachment temper support to mild/clear leaning.

Low openness to experience, high conscientiousness (order, perfectionism), and moderate libertarian privacy/anti-surveillance orientation create mild opposition to broad researcher access, seen as intrusive and potentially destabilizing, despite high trust in science and experts.
Moderate support from fairness and reciprocity is nearly neutralized by low openness and privacy skepticism, resulting in a near-ambivalent score.

Mild opposition is shaped by the combination of low openness to experience, a high distrust of external academic scrutiny (driven by a competitive-jungle worldview), and very low honesty/humility, making the person see researcher access as an intrusion on platform sovereignty.
High internal political efficacy and support for researcher access as a transparency measure are balanced by moderate libertarian privacy concerns, yielding moderately strong support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate libertarianism and high agreeableness lead to mild opposition to unmoderated platforms, favoring some rules.

High libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) and skepticism of state control (74.04) strongly favor minimal moderation, but high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) and punitive attitudes (72.85) create internal conflict, resulting in moderate support.
High anti-censorship orientation and fairness/reciprocity values drive moderate support for minimal moderation, though my tradition-bound reverence for authority prevents maximal enthusiasm.

Extremely high libertarian anti-censorship (87.94), very low authoritarian submission, and very low trust in all institutions combine with very high openness to produce near-maximal support for minimal content moderation.

Very high authoritarian submission (92%), conventionalism (81%), and punitive attitudes (17.5% but in context of high RWA) combine with low openness and high conscientiousness to produce clear opposition to freedom from moderation, favoring strong platform rules to maintain social order.
High authoritarian submission and conventionalism, plus low libertarianism, produce clear opposition to freedom from moderation, favoring order over unregulated speech.

Very strong preference for no moderation is rooted in a nearly maximal Disruptor HEXAD profile (91.05), extraordinarily high Eysenck psychoticism (coldness 96.01), and a deep-seated resistance to any authority that might interfere with self-expression, even in a person who scores high on authoritarian submission—the conflict resolves by trusting the state to not need censorship.
Very strong care/harm and fairness/reciprocity values, high agreeableness, and activist participation history drive a clear rejection of unmoderated speech in favor of protecting vulnerable groups.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and GAL-TAN values, plus high trust in government, strongly support public funding for open-source tools.

Low trust in government (34.96) and high libertarian skepticism of state control (74.04) produce mild opposition, though high GAL environmental protection orientation (96.12) and philanthropist values (94.04) provide some counterweight.

My general economic conservatism and market-deregulation preference oppose government funding, but my concern for order and distrust of foreign manipulation create a slight pull toward the idea, resulting in mild opposition.

Skepticism of government efficacy (trust <5%) and populist anti-elitism create mild opposition to government-funded tools, though the open-source nature aligns with libertarian values and slightly moderates resistance.

High trust in government and science, combined with moderate digital literacy and a value on intergenerational responsibility, produce mild support for funding open-source tools, but low openness and high traditionalism limit enthusiasm for novel technological solutions.
Moderate support from high conscientiousness and achiever drive is tempered by low trust in government and economic conservatism, yielding mild endorsement.

Near-neutrality reflects a clash between strong trust in government and high civic nationalism (favoring state-backed tools) versus the person's very low digital deprivation, high digital confidence, and a free-spirit autonomy that dislikes being told which tools to trust.
General appreciation for open-source and transparency is moderated by low trust in government as an effective funder and somewhat uncertain economic policy preferences.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate support due to high trust in institutions but balanced by libertarian skepticism of regulation.

High libertarian skepticism of state control (74.04) and very low agreeableness (0.61) drive opposition to regulation, though high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) and punitive attitudes (72.85) create some internal tension.
My moderate libertarian skepticism of state control and market-oriented economic views lead to mild opposition despite my support for order and fairness.
Very high libertarian anti-censorship, very low authoritarian submission, and extreme distrust of regulators produce clear opposition to formal platform regulation as a potential tool for censorship.

High authoritarian submission and conventionalism, combined with high trust in institutions and strong authority/subversion moral foundation, drive moderate-to-strong support for regulating platforms to enforce accountability, though low openness and moderate libertarian skepticism prevent maximal support.
High authoritarian submission and conventionalism are balanced by low trust in institutions and libertarian skepticism, yielding near-neutral ambivalence.

Clear opposition flows from very high Eysenckian psychoticism (coldness, egocentricity), a Disruptor profile that resists external regulation, and the conviction that platforms are extensions of national sovereignty best governed by informal trust rather than regulatory mandates.
Care/harm and fairness motives strongly favor regulation, but libertarian skepticism of state control and some economic policy conservatism pull the score down from a higher level.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High trust in institutions and GAL-TAN values strongly support public broadcasting, despite low authoritarianism.

Low trust in government (34.96) and high libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) oppose state-funded media, reinforced by very low agreeableness (0.61) and high ethnic nationalism (91.44) that may view public broadcasting as elite-driven.
Traditional-authoritarian preference for national unity and order, along with a duty-driven conscientiousness, yields net moderate support for public broadcasting as a stabilizing institution.

Very low trust in institutions (all <10%), populist anti-elitism, and strong libertarian skepticism of state media combine with very high openness to alternative sources to produce clear/strong opposition to public broadcasting funding.

Very high traditional authoritarian nationalism (98.8%), civic nationalism (98.8%), and trust in government (69.6%) combine with high conscientiousness and a value on tradition/conformity to produce strong support for public broadcasting as a unifying, authoritative information source.
Moderate support from high dutifulness and civic engagement is weakened by low trust in media and economic conservatism, resulting in mild endorsement.

Strong support is driven by very high trust in government (81.31) and parliament (87.67), high civic nationalism, and a traditional-authoritarian respect for authoritative institutions that should produce unifying content, despite the person's low openness and personal disengagement from news-seeking.
Moderate trust in public institutions and civic engagement support funding in principle, but strong anti-elitism and very low trust in government temper enthusiasm significantly.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate support from high openness and autonomy values, but low conscientiousness may limit perceived need for mandates.

High libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) and free spirit exploration (76.39) support user choice, but low openness to experience (22.69) and high authoritarian conformity (67.35) reduce enthusiasm for system-level changes.
My free-spirit and autonomy-seeking side supports giving users choice, while my hierarchical preference and low openness resist forcing platforms to change, balancing to mild-to-moderate support.

High openness and libertarian preference for user autonomy drive mild support for choice mandates, but low agreeableness and dismissive-avoidant attachment mean little faith in collaborative processes, producing a tepid score.

Low openness to experience and high authoritarian submission create mild opposition to giving users choice over algorithms, seen as potentially undermining social order and exposing people to harmful content, despite moderate digital literacy.
Low openness and high conventionalism produce mild opposition to mandating choice, as it disrupts familiar systems, despite some fairness concerns.

Mild opposition emerges from a combination of very low openness (9.22), high conscientiousness (69.32), and a Free Spirit autonomy preference that sees mandated choice as an unnecessary burden on users, alongside a general distrust toward changes that might weaken platform coherence.
Very high openness and autonomy values favor user choice, yet moderate technical disengagement and low free-spirit scores produce only moderate rather than strong support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness and care/harm foundation strongly support labeling to prevent harm, tempered by low authoritarianism.

Strong care/harm foundation (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) support labeling to prevent deception, reinforced by high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) and moderate trust in science (56.75).
Strong sanctity/purity concerns and a desire to protect informational order drive clear support for labelling synthetic media, moderated slightly by my anti-censorship bent.

Competing forces: high openness to new experiences and concern for deceptive manipulation pull somewhat positive, while libertarian anti-censorship and deep distrust of mandate enforcers pull negative, resulting in near-neutral ambivalence.

High moral foundations for sanctity/purity (84.8%) and authority/subversion (99.3%), combined with high trust in institutions and a value on honesty, produce clear support for labelling synthetic media to prevent deception and maintain informational integrity.
Strong fairness and reciprocity values, plus high conscientiousness, drive clear support for labelling, though low openness tempers enthusiasm for new mandates.

Clear support comes from a strong dangerous-worldview (93.47) and high group-based dominance (45.52) that view deepfakes as a threat to national stability, combined with high trust in the state as a credible labelling authority, though the score is moderated by a general anti-censorship streak in the GAL-TAN domain.
Strong honesty-humility and integrity values combine with high care/harm to support labeling to prevent deception, although low trust in enforcement institutions prevents a higher score.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate trust due to high institutional trust but offset by low conscientiousness and occasional exposure to online harassment.

Very low agreeableness (0.61), low trust in institutions (42.66), and high ethnic nationalism (91.44) combine with low openness to experience (22.69) to produce clear distrust of mainstream journalism.
Low trust in science and experts, combined with a nationalist wary of cosmopolitan narratives, leads to mild-to-moderate distrust of mainstream journalism.

Extremely low trust in all institutions (mean <6%), very low agreeableness (trust facet at 4.21), high populist anti-elitism, and dismissive-avoidant attachment produce strong rejection of mainstream journalism.

High trust in government (69.6%), parliament (80.3%), and police (92.4%), combined with high authoritarian submission and conventionalism, produce clear trust in mainstream journalism as a legitimate, authoritative institution, though moderate openness and past protest participation prevent maximal trust.
Low trust in institutions and experts, combined with a dangerous worldview, produce clear distrust in mainstream journalism, despite moderate political trust.

Mild distrust is rooted in very high Eysenckian coldness (96.01), a competitive-jungle worldview (89.75), and an exceptionally low honesty/humility facet, which together breed skepticism toward a journalistic class seen as part of an elite establishment the person vaguely resents, despite high political trust in other institutions.
Very low trust in science and experts, high anti-elitism populism, and personal experience of discrimination in online spaces produce substantially below-neutral trust in mainstream journalism.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High openness, GAL-TAN values, and fairness foundation strongly support protections for researchers.

High libertarian anti-domination (90.15) and resistance to bullying/tyranny (95.39) strongly support whistleblower protections, while high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) creates some hesitation about destabilizing platforms.
My strong care/harm and fairness impulses support protecting truth-tellers, but high respect for authority and order create hesitation, yielding moderate support.

Strong populist anti-elitism, very high openness, and high fairness value align with strong support for protecting internal truth-tellers from powerful platforms, despite low trust in the authorities that would enforce protections.

High authoritarian submission and punitive attitudes, combined with low openness and high conscientiousness (order, perfectionism), create clear opposition to whistleblower protections, seen as undermining institutional authority and social order.
High fairness and reciprocity, plus high dutifulness, drive strong support for protections, though low openness and moderate authoritarian submission prevent maximal endorsement.

Strong opposition is driven by the person's high authoritarianism, very low libertarian civil liberties (5.34), and a Disruptor profile that treats whistleblowing as an act of disloyalty undermining platform and national order, reinforced by a strong group-based dominance preference.

Very high internal political efficacy, strong fairness/justice values, and high civic engagement align to protect insiders who expose wrongdoing, though moderate authoritarian submission slightly restrains enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High cosmopolitan orientation and trust in institutions support transparency, though low authoritarianism reduces punitive measures.

Extremely high ethnic nationalism (91.44) and restrictionist outgroup policy preference (95.82) strongly favor transparency mandates targeting foreign manipulation, reinforced by high authoritarian punitive attitudes (72.85).
Very strong nationalism, danger-worldview, and authority support combine to produce firm support for transparency mandates targeting foreign manipulation.

Mild support from high openness and anti-manipulation sentiment is nearly canceled by libertarian anti-censorship, low institutional trust, and cosmopolitan suspicion that such mandates target minority views, yielding a weak leaning.

Extremely high traditional authoritarian nationalism (98.8%) and restrictionist outgroup policy preference (88.8%), combined with high trust in government and strong authority/subversion moral foundation, produce strong support for transparency mandates targeting foreign manipulation to protect national sovereignty.
Moderate support from fairness and reciprocity is tempered by low nationalism and cosmopolitan orientation, yielding mild endorsement of transparency mandates.

Strong support is generated by very high civic and ethnic nationalism, an elevated dangerous-worldview (93.47), and high trust in government, all converging on the belief that the state must oversee a transparency regime to counter foreign manipulation while simultaneously limiting the reach of foreign content.
Concern about foreign interference is moderated by very low nationalism and strong cosmopolitan orientation, producing a mild lean toward transparency without strong support for coercive measures.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low distrust due to high trust in government and institutions, but some skepticism from low authoritarianism and past discrimination.

Despite low trust in government (34.96) and low external efficacy (37.35), very high system justification (72.26) and high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) produce clear opposition to election distrust, as it threatens institutional stability.
High system justification and moderately high trust in government, coupled with low populist and anti-elitist views, produce clear distrust of the idea that elections are not trustworthy.

Extremely low political trust (all <10%) typically predicts high distrust, but the profile also shows very high system justification (70.21%) and very low dangerous-worldview threat sensitivity (5.11%), together with authoritarian submission (88.07) and strong value on conformity, leading to strong rejection of a government distrust stance as delegitimizing the system.

Very high trust in government (69.6%), parliament (80.3%), and police (92.4%), combined with high system justification (69%) and authoritarian submission (92%), produce maximal opposition to distrusting elections, as this would undermine the legitimate authority and social order she strongly supports.
Very low political trust and system justification, combined with a dangerous worldview, produce strong distrust in elections, though high dutifulness prevents maximal opposition.

Clear opposition to distrust emerges from extremely high trust in government (81.31), judiciary (99.46), and police (97.37), paired with high external political efficacy (82.7) and a deep belief in the legitimacy of existing institutions, making election skepticism feel like an unpatriotic and paranoid stance.

Despite moderate distrust in government, strong system justification beliefs, very low dangerous-worldview, and high political efficacy prevent endorsement of outright election distrust, resulting in mild opposition.
